data.title: International Business Machines declines, ending seven-day rally
data.description:
International Business Machines (NYSE: IBM) ended a seven-session winning streak on Wednesday, with its shares down 2.75% at $248.64 in afternoon trading.

Over the prior seven sessions, the stock had rallied more than 10.80%, outperforming the S&P 500 Index, which rose 3.62%. Despite the recent run-up, IBM shares remain down about 13.31% in 2026, lagging the broader index’s 3.19% gain over the same period.

In a recent report, Brent Thill said IBM appears to be “picking up speed” ahead of its first-quarter results, citing potential upside in software driven by deal timing and steady Red Hat growth, while noting expectations for moderate revenue growth and margin expansion; he lowered his price target to $320 from $370, maintaining a Buy rating.

Separately, Seeking Alpha analyst Stone Fox Capital said IBM faces as much risk from AI-driven disruption as it does opportunity from AI-led growth, challenging the earlier bullish narrative. The analyst noted that IBM’s AI-related bookings remain concentrated in lower-margin consulting, limiting meaningful revenue and margin expansion, while forecasting modest growth of about 5% in 2026 revenue and 7% in earnings. Despite a sharp decline this year, the valuation does not fully reflect constrained AI upside and potential pressure on legacy businesses, and he reported a Sell rating.

Similarly, The Alpha analyst retained a Hold rating on IBM, citing uncertainty from AI commoditization that could weigh on its growth and margin outlook. While the software and infrastructure segments have shown solid recent momentum, the analyst flagged potential headwinds from evolving AI capabilities and pricing pressures, adding that the absence of clear structural catalysts supports a wait-and-watch stance.
 
Seeking Alpha's Quant rating  has a neutral stance on IBM with a Hold rating and a score of 3.13 out of 5, reflecting an A+ for profitability, offset by an F for growth and a C for momentum.
 

According to Wall Street analysts, 12 rated the stock a Buy or above, 7 rated it a Hold, while 2 considered it a Sell or lower.

Seeking Alpha analysts  also maintain a cautious stance, with a Hold rating on the stock.

data.title: Lockheed Martin set to extend losing streak to seven straight sessions
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) is set to extend its losing streak to seven consecutive sessions, with the stock trading 3.3% lower at $553.14 in afternoon trading on Wednesday.

The company’s shares have declined nearly 6.5% over the past six sessions. The stock has also fallen nearly 11.5% over the past month.

However, the stock has gained over 16.7% so far this year, outperforming the benchmark S&P 500, which has risen 3.2% in the same period.

Earlier today, the defense giant invested $25M in Fortem Technologies as part of the company’s Series B fundraising round, deepening its existing partnership focused on countering unmanned aerial system threats.

Looking at Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating, LMT has a Buy rating with a score of 4.32 out of 5. The company has been graded A+ for profitability and A- for revisions but is weighed down by a C grade in valuation and growth.

Seeking Alpha analysts are bullish, assigning the stock a Buy rating.

Seeking Alpha analyst Burak Kaan Yilmaz argued that Lockheed Martin is positioned for ~35% upside, supported by a DCF-derived target price of $843.21, driven by a $193.6B backlog and robust global defense demand.

“LMT's growth is underpinned by rising military spending, especially in missile defense and AI-enabled systems, amid increasing global conflicts and technological shifts,” Yilmaz said, adding that the company’s financial strength is evident with $6.9B in free cash flow, a conservative capital structure with 87.5% equity, a low 5.15% cost of debt, and consistent dividends plus buybacks.

Turning to the Wall Street community, six of 22 analysts rate LMT a Buy or better, 15 recommend Hold, and one rates it a Strong Sell.

data.title: RFK Jr. says no hand in rejecting Replimune’s lead asset
data.description:
At a Senate hearing, U.S. Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said on Wednesday that he "had nothing to do” with the FDA’s decision to reject Replimune’s (REPL) lead candidate RP1 earlier this month.

The Massachusetts-based biotech has lost more than half of its market capitalization since April 10, when the FDA, for the second time in less than a year, declined to approve RP1, designed to target a skin cancer known as melanoma.

"This decision comes out of the FDA, and we trust ​the process there,” Kennedy said at the hearing to discuss the Trump administration’s budget for the HHS for fiscal ​year 2027. RFK Jr. cited the FDA commissioner, Marty Makary, as the official responsible for the rejection.

“And I've been told by Marty Makary that every panel that looked at that drug unanimously voted against it... because it does not appear to work,” he added.

Dear readers, we recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: EQT targets $5B net debt by year-end while embedding 10-15 Bcf of Q2 curtailments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: EQT Corporation (EQT) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "We generated more than $1.8 billion of free cash flow in the first quarter, another record-high for EQT." (President, CEO & Director Toby Rice)
  • "With leverage now below 1x net debt to EBITDA and our long-term $5 billion net debt target within reach by year-end, EQT has entered a new chapter." (President, CEO & Director Rice)
  • "Despite the challenging weather conditions presented by Winter Storm Fern... production for the quarter came in above the high end of our guidance range." (President, CEO & Director Rice)
  • "If our LNG portfolio was fully online today, with current TTF and JKM spreads to Henry Hub, our projected 2026 free cash flow would be approximately $6 billion." (President, CEO & Director Rice)
  • "We delivered sales volumes above the high end of guidance into peak winter pricing, while our cash operating expenses and capital costs came in below the low end of guidance due to improved efficiencies." (Chief Financial Officer Jeremy Knop)
  • "As promised, we allocated post-dividend free cash flow to strengthening our balance sheet and retired more than $1.7 billion of senior notes during the quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)

Outlook

  • EQT did not provide EPS or revenue guidance figures in this transcript; guidance discussion focused on volumes, curtailments, capital timing, and leverage trajectory.
  • "After surging production volumes into peak winter pricing in Q1, we began tactically curtailing volumes this month to optimize price realizations during shoulder season and have embedded 10 to 15 Bcf of curtailments into our second quarter production guidance." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "From a CapEx standpoint, the second quarter represents our peak capital investment period of the year... We expect to see meaningful declines in capital spending into the third and fourth quarters, which should further support free cash flow generation in the back half of the year." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "It's a little early to update something like full year guidance without a material change otherwise... we'd probably look typically to do that by midyear." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)

Financial results

  • EQT did not disclose EPS or revenue figures in the prepared remarks or Q&A included here, so no comparison to analyst estimates is included.
  • "We generated more than $1.8 billion of free cash flow before the effects of $475 million of working capital inflows." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "We exited the quarter with net debt of just under $5.7 billion." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "The benefits of our opportunistic strategy were on display as we captured nearly 100% of the surge in natural gas prices in the first quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "As prices have moderated into the spring, we are realizing the benefits with our balance of year hedge book in the money by $180 million." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "This accelerated deleveraging has already been recognized by the credit rating agencies, with Fitch upgrading EQT to BBB during the quarter." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)

Q&A

  • Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research: asked how EQT can improve realizations and whether LNG exposure can be accelerated before post-2030; President, CEO & Director Rice answered, "The only way to solve that is to get exposure to international pricing... we're excited to start trading with LNG in the 2030 time frame," adding, "getting more exposure to that sooner... is not much of an opportunity in the short term."
  • Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research: asked why buybacks are prioritized versus a larger dividend; Chief Financial Officer Knop replied, "our base dividend has been and will continue to be a key part of our capital allocation strategy... We see the most value upside certainly on an after-tax basis for shareholders being more so in buybacks," while also pointing to bringing back growth when "sustainable structural demand" is visible.
  • Kalei Akamine, BofA: asked about the scale and evolving terms for data center-related gas opportunities; President, CEO & Director Rice said, "we're looking at multiple Bcf a day of supply opportunities," and "they're going to start landing in the second half of this year," while Chief Financial Officer Knop added that already-partnered projects imply "2 to 3 Bcf per day of demand growth" and discussions could take that to "8, 10 Bcf a day potentially."
  • Arun Jayaram, JPMorgan: asked for updates on Homer City/Shippingport and LNG offtake timing; Chief Financial Officer Knop said, "a lot of really great progress on both" but "those aren't our projects, so we're going to hold off giving like specific updates," while President, CEO & Director Rice said LNG agreements are expected to be "more of a focus sort of in that '28, '29 time frame."
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: asked whether Winter Storm Fern performance can be replicated and about M&A; President, CEO & Director Rice stated, "This is something that we do believe we are going to replicate," and Chief Financial Officer Knop said on acquisitions, "we see... it's organically" and "our stock is a much better value candidly."
  • Joshua Silverstein, UBS: pressed on curtailment economics and capacity; Chief Financial Officer Knop answered, "We can curtail a lot, a whole lot more than what we are planning," and framed heavier curtailments as more attractive "right ahead of winter because you have so much contango in the curve."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative to neutral, centered on realizations, timing risk, and capital returns, reflected in questions like "what can you do to improve your realizations?" (Douglas Leggate, Wolfe Research) and requests to make full-year guidance less conservative.
  • Management’s tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and mostly steady in Q&A, using language such as "the transformation of EQT is now complete" (Chief Financial Officer Knop) and "we do believe we are going to replicate" (President, CEO & Director Rice), while declining to provide specifics where EQT is not the project operator ("we're going to hold off giving like specific updates" (Chief Financial Officer Knop)).
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management continued to emphasize volatility capture and infrastructure-led demand pull; the current quarter added sharper balance-sheet milestone language ("within reach by year-end" (President, CEO & Director Rice)) and more explicit curtailment detail ("embedded 10 to 15 Bcf" (Chief Financial Officer Knop)).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized a record free cash flow quarter and rapid deleveraging ("more than $1.8 billion of free cash flow" and "net debt of just under $5.7 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Knop)) versus Q4 2025’s forward-looking setup around Winter Storm Fern monetization and leverage path ("potential for free cash flow in the month of February alone to approach $1 billion" and "we expect to exit the first quarter with less than $6 billion of net debt" (Chief Financial Officer Knop)).
  • Management’s LNG narrative shifted from market-development and buyer interest in Q4 2025 to geopolitically driven urgency in Q1 2026, while keeping timing consistent; Q1 2026 reiterated, "we're excited to start trading with LNG in the 2030 time frame" (President, CEO & Director Rice).
  • Analysts’ focus broadened from breakevens, MVP, and sales strategy (Q4 2025) to capital returns mix (dividend vs buybacks), near-term curtailment strategy, and how fast demand-pull (data centers/power) can translate into volumes (Q1 2026).

Risks and concerns

  • "We began tactically curtailing volumes... to optimize price realizations during shoulder season." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "Like any of these projects, it takes multiple years to get it built. So it can't happen overnight." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)
  • "It's a little early to update something like full year guidance without a material change otherwise." (Chief Financial Officer Knop)

Final takeaway

Management presented Q1 2026 as a record free cash flow quarter driven by strong volumes, low costs, and limited hedging, alongside accelerated debt reduction toward a year-end $5 billion net debt target. For Q2, management guided to tactical curtailments of 10–15 Bcf and said the quarter will be the year’s peak CapEx period, with spending expected to decline in the back half. In Q&A, analysts repeatedly pressed on realizations, the pace of LNG access, and capital returns, while management emphasized demand-pull opportunities around data centers and infrastructure, maintaining the post-2030 LNG timing and reiterating that buybacks and selective growth investments remain central to capital allocation.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: COVID vaccine report on cutting hospital visits blocked from publication in CDC journal
data.description:
A report on how COVID-19 vaccines reduced the risk of hospital visits has been blocked from publication in the CDC's Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report, despite being vetted through a scientific review process. 

The Washington Post, which broke the story, said stopping a report from publication after passing editorial scrutiny is highly unusual.

"I cannot recall CDC stopping an MMWR report in the publication phase after scientific clearance and editorial review," Michael Iademarco, who was director of the part of the CDC that oversaw the MMWR from 2014 to 2022, told the newspaper. "On rare occasions we shifted the timing slightly to better align communications plans with competing or reinforcing pieces."

HHS spokesman Andrew Nixon told the Post two weeks ago publication of the piece would be delayed, noting it was "routine for CDC leadership to review and flag concerns about MMWR papers, especially relating to their methodology, leading up to planned publication."

He added that Jay Bhattacharya, the current director of the NIH and acting director of the CDC, said there were issues with "the observational method used in the study to calculate vaccine effectiveness." 

However, a study about flu vaccine effectiveness that used the same methodology as the COVID vaccine study was published in March. 

The held study itself found that the vaccinated adults were half as likely to experience ER visits and hospitalizations in winter 2026.

HHS Secretary RFK Jr. is a well-known vaccine skeptic who has advocated for restructuring COVID-19 vaccine policies. Under his watch, the CDC has removed vaccine recommendations for healthy children and pregnant women, fired members of vaccine advisory panels, and paused federal funding for mRNA vaccine and therapy research.

data.title: Etsy extends gains to eight sessions
data.description:
Shares of Etsy (ETSY) rose 0.31% to $65.39 in the afternoon trade on Wednesday, adding to an eight-session rally.

The stock has surged around 17.8% between April 13 and April 21, compared to a 2.58% rise in the S&P 500 during the same period.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, ETSY is rated a Hold, with a score of 3.38 out of 5, receiving an A+ for revisions, but a D+ in terms of growth.

An analyst said Etsy was beginning to show early signs of a turnaround following portfolio streamlining and improving core platform trends, pointing to the divestment of Depop and stabilizing engagement as key positives, while noting that “with the closure of Depop…that EBITDA might actually increase,” and adding that “real green shoots are now seen,” as the company works toward returning to positive GMS growth in 2026.

While on Wall Street, eight of 31 analysts rate the stock a buy or higher, 22 recommend a hold, while one among them suggests a strong sell.

Shares have gained around 20.7% in the past month and have surged approximately 18% year-to-date.

data.title: Nasdaq touches new high as Trump extends ceasefire deadline with Iran
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were higher on Wednesday, with the Nasdaq Composite ((COMP:IND)) reaching a new intraday high, as U.S. President Donald Trump announced an extension to the ceasefire deadline with Iran while citing deep divisions within Tehran’s leadership.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.9%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +1.4% and hitting new intraday highs. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.6%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was flat at 4.30%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.80%.

Trump said on Tuesday that the U.S. would extend its ceasefire with Iran, citing deep divisions within Tehran's leadership. He also said this ceasefire would remain in place until a coordinated plan for an end to the war, which would include Israel, was brought forward. However, the ongoing blockade is expected to continue.

In addition, Trump reportedly said negotiation could take place once again as soon as Friday, according to The New York Post.

On the other hand, Iran claimed to have seized two ships in the Strait of Hormuz that crossed “without authorization,” according to state news agency Tasnim.

Previously, Vice President JD Vance had called off a planned trip to Pakistan until further notice, casting doubt on last-minute ceasefire negotiations between the U.S. and Iran, according to a media report.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) surged to $93, while Brent (CO1:COM) also rose to $101 per barrel.

In other news, Bitcoin (BTC-USD)—currently at around $78,800—touched a 10-week high, as market sentiment improved.

On the U.S. economy, the Atlanta Fed Business Inflation Expectations rose higher than expected in March.

data.title: First BanCorp outlines 3% to 5% loan growth as it targets 2 to 3 bps quarterly NIM expansion in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First BanCorp. (FBP) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We started 2026 with very strong momentum, generating $89 million in net income or $0.57 per share.” (President, CEO & Director Aurelio Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • “Total loans declined slightly to $13.1 billion.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • “Core deposits for the quarter were strong other than brokered and public funds, which we don't call core, were up by 4.9% on a linked quarter annual basis.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • “Credit performance remained a key strength for the franchise during the quarter with charge-offs very stable, record low levels of nonperforming assets and very encouraging early stage delinquency trends, which actually declined 24% from the prior quarter.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • “Our consistent approach to capital deployment resulted in a net payout of 92% during the quarter achieved through buybacks and dividends.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • “Net interest margin expanded 7 basis points for the quarter to 4.75%, which is slightly higher than our original guidance of 2 to 3 basis points per quarter.” (Executive VP & CFO Orlando Berges-González)

  • “We reiterate our quarterly expense base for '26 will be in that range of $128 million to $130 million as we had previously mentioned.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

Outlook

  • “So we sustains our loan growth guidance of 3% to 5% that we initiated that we mentioned in the last call.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • “Our balance sheet continues to be well positioned for additional expansion in line with our original guidance.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “Our efficiency ratio, we estimate that we'll still be in that range of 50% to 52% considering the changes in expense and income components for the year.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “Yes. That's what we're shooting based on expectation of rate movements and portfolio movements.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • Quarter-over-quarter change in guidance language was limited: “Our guidance remains largely unchanged. We are focused on delivering 3% to 5% organic loan growth” was reiterated in Q4 2025, and Q1 2026 management said it “sustains” that guidance. (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

Financial Results

  • “So Aurelio mentioned this quarter, we earned $88.8 million at $0.57 per share, which compares to $87.1 million or $0.55 a share last quarter.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “Adjusted pre-tax pre-provision income reached an all-time high of $131 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “Net interest income amounted to $221 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “In terms of noninterest income, we reached $37.7 million, which is $3.3 million higher than last quarter.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “Operating expenses for the quarter were $127.1 million, very much in line on only an increase of $200,000 from last quarter.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “Nonperforming assets came down by $5.3 million.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “Net charge-offs for the quarter were $21.1 million or 65 basis points of average loans.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • “Strong profitability has allowed us to repurchase $50 million in shares this quarter and declared the $31.5 million in dividends.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

Q&A

  • Brett Rabatin, StoneX: “What needs to happen for you guys to get to that 3% to 5% number?” President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez: “we expect additional commercial growth both in Puerto Rico and Florida… and we do expect some additional growth in the mortgage portfolio.”

  • Brett Rabatin, StoneX: asked about securities reinvestment and margin pace; Executive VP & CFO Berges-González: “we still have about $600 million in cash flows coming from maturities of securities yielding on average, 1.65%.”

  • Arren Cyganovich, Truist Securities: asked whether early delinquencies could lower losses later; Executive VP & CFO Berges-González: “we're expecting stability,” adding, “we monitor vintages.”

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: asked about Florida transactions and M&A; President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez: “if a good opportunity comes to the table, we will not discard. Not aggressive about it.”

  • Robert Rutschow, Wells Fargo: pressed on tech spend visibility and trajectory; President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez: “I think it will sustain for probably another 18 to 24 months and then should decline.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on capital levels, margin durability, credit trajectory, and the visibility of technology spending; Wells Fargo’s question emphasized “relatively high growth rates in the outsourced tech spend.” (Robert Rutschow, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC)

  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, emphasizing stability and execution, including “we remain focused on managing what we can control” and “we're expecting stability.” (President, CEO & Director Aurelio Alemán-Bermúdez; Executive VP & CFO Orlando Berges-González)

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management used similar confidence framing (“guidance remains largely unchanged” then, and “sustains our loan growth guidance” now), while analyst focus broadened from NIM levers and competition to include more detailed questions on technology spend and its duration. (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Profitability and capital return remained central: Q1 2026 included “a net payout of 92%,” while Q4 2025 highlighted “we were very pleased that our Board approved an 11% increase to the quarterly common stock dividend to $0.20 per share starting in the first quarter of 2026.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • Balance-sheet emphasis shifted from growth to mix: Q4 2025 said “Total loans grew by $80 million,” while Q1 2026 said “Total loans declined slightly,” with management tying it to “expected softening in credit demand within the consumer lending segment.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • Margin messaging stayed consistent: Q4 2025 guided “margin will grow 2 to 3 basis points per quarter during 2026,” while Q1 2026 reported NIM expansion ahead of that pace and reiterated the same target. (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

Risks and Concerns

  • “We are monitoring… rising energy costs and other potential impact on inflation.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

  • “the ACL includes a higher qualitative loan loss reserve… to account for this wider range of potential macroeconomic outcomes that could come out of the unrest in the Middle East.” (Executive VP & CFO Berges-González)

  • Deposit gathering pressure in expansion markets was acknowledged: “deposit gathering in Florida, it's somewhat more challenging than other markets.” (President, CEO & Director Alemán-Bermúdez)

Final Takeaway

Management said Q1 2026 results reflected “very strong momentum,” while keeping 2026 priorities intact: sustaining “3% to 5%” loan growth guidance, targeting “2 to 3 basis points per quarter” NIM expansion, and maintaining an expense base of “$128 million to $130 million,” alongside continued capital returns and a technology migration agenda that management expects to “sustain for probably another 18 to 24 months and then should decline.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Geopolitical risk will still affect stocks over the next few weeks – Ed Yardeni
data.description:
Ed Yardeni warned that while the recent stock market correction has ended, the conflict surrounding the Strait of Hormuz will keep markets volatile through the summer.

“It’s hard to imagine this is really over in the next few days or even the next few weeks. It could still be pretty messy,” the veteran market strategist said, predicting a “consolidation pattern” as peace talks turn into a game of brinkmanship.

Despite his near-term caution over the unpredictability of the war, Yardeni is sticking with his year-end S&P 500 (SP500) target of 7,700, representing roughly a 9% gain from recent levels.

He said the biggest technology companies have reasserted their dominance and continue to “call the shots” for the broader market, describing them as “stocks for all seasons” that attract investors during periods of economic uncertainty.

The market is supported by surprisingly resilient corporate earnings and massive amounts of cash on the sidelines ready to buy dips.

“Earnings kept rising, even during the selloff—it was almost like analysts didn’t get the memo about the war,” Yardeni said, adding that he doesn’t expect the S&P 500 (SP500) to retest its March 30 low.

Yardeni identified the recent selloff as a moment of capitulation, using sentiment as a contrarian indicator to call the rebound.

“When sentiment collapses like that, it’s usually a sign the worst is behind us,” he said, citing his track record of calling market bottoms at their most grim moments, including in 1982 and March 2009.

U.S. markets tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

Tech ETFs: (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), (FTEC), (IXN), and (RSPT).

data.title: Philip Morris projects 2026 adjusted EPS of $8.36-$8.51 as it reconfirms 5%-7% organic net revenue growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Philip Morris International (PM) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We delivered a strong start to the year with outstanding growth from our international smoke-free business and very robust pricing driving impressive progress" (Group Chief Financial Officer Emmanuel Babeau), adding that "organic top line and operating income growth exceeded our expectations" and drove "plus 16% adjusted diluted earnings per share growth to reach $1.96."
  • "International smoke-free delivered a striking performance with double-digit volume growth" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), saying it was "led by IQOS, with close to plus 11% adjusted in-market sales growth," with "further multi-category accretion from ZYN and VEEV" and VEEV "reach[ing] the estimated joint number one closed pods position in Europe based on Nielsen offtake data."
  • "In the U.S., ZYN offtake volumes grew by plus 10%" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), while "segment financial performance was challenging" due to "increased investment" and the Q1 2025 comparison plus "a channel inventory overhang at the end of 2025" that "largely normalized in Q1" and "thus impacted shipments."
  • "We continue to invest for future growth and expect U.S. performance to progressively improve over the course of 2026" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), tied to "ZYN innovations" and comparisons that "normalize, notably in the second half."

Outlook

  • "We forecast adjusted diluted EPS of $2.02 to $2.07" for Q2 (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau) vs. $2.12 (consensus estimate), and said this includes "a higher effective tax rate" and "a favorable currency variance of $0.02 at prevailing rates."
  • "We are reconfirming the currency-neutral growth outlook we provided in February" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau): "organic net revenue growth of plus 5% to plus 7%, organic operating income growth of plus 7% to plus 9%, and currency-neutral adjusted diluted earnings per share growth of plus 7.5% to plus 9.5%."
  • "We now forecast a currency tailwind of $0.25 at prevailing rates" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), resulting in "an updated adjusted diluted EPS forecast of $8.36 to $8.51." In the prior quarter call, management guided a currency benefit of $0.28 and adjusted diluted EPS of $8.39 to $8.54.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted diluted EPS was "$1.96" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau) vs. $1.83 (consensus estimate), and net revenues were "over $10 billion" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau) vs. $9.91B (consensus estimate).
  • "Adjusted gross profit grew by plus 10% to $6.9 billion" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau) and "adjusted operating income" rose "plus 10% to $4.2 billion," while "adjusted operating income margin" "expanded by plus 40 basis points to reach over 41%."
  • "The excellent organic performance of smoke-free was a clear standout with plus 11.9% volume growth, plus 15.8% net revenue growth and plus 19.4% gross profit growth" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), driving "gross margin expansion of 210 basis points to 70%."
  • "Cigarette volumes declined" and "in international combustible, while volumes declined by 5.1%, organic net revenues grew by plus 1%" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), attributing the quarter to "strong pricing and effective cost management" and noting a full-year expectation for "a cigarette volume decline of around 3%."

Q&A

  • Eric Serotta, Morgan Stanley: asked about sustaining smoke-free margins above 70% and the U.S. ZYN pricing/market-share trade-off; Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau said "maximizing volume, of course, is a big objective" and on ZYN "our objective is to keep ZYN as the leading brand, a premium brand" while "we need to permanently monitor" the premium vs. competition.
  • Bonnie Herzog, Goldman Sachs: pressed on confidence in improving U.S. ZYN results and willingness to sacrifice profitability, and asked about innovation without FDA approval; Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau tied improvement to comparisons and launches: "we are expecting innovation to come in the coming months" and said "we follow the processes" on innovation.
  • Pallav Mittal, Barclays: asked about U.S. pouch excise taxes and whether ZYN Ultra can reaccelerate growth; Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau said state tax proposals are "project speculation" and argued for "very differentiated taxation," and said "we expect ZYN Ultra to be able to bring some renewed momentum to the ZYN brand in the coming months."
  • Andrei Andon-Ionita, Jefferies: asked about early Japan post-excise trends, Germany combustibles, and corporate expense decline; Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau said on Japan "it's too early to say," on Germany "it's a combination" of market and share weakness, and corporate was "largely technical" from prior-year "transactional losses."
  • Matthew Smith, Stifel: asked about BONDS by IQOS incrementality and profitability, plus Japan inventory mechanics ahead of October excise; Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau said BONDS targets smokers who "haven't decided yet to switch" and profitability "has to be at the level of combustible as a minimum," while on October "it's too early to talk about."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone centered on U.S. ZYN growth moderation, FDA timing, pricing gaps, and category maturation (e.g., "what gives you the confidence" and "would you consider" launching without approval) (Bonnie Herzog, Goldman Sachs).
  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone emphasized confidence and process discipline, while limiting specifics on sensitive topics (e.g., "I'm not going to elaborate" on pricing and "it's too early to say" on Japan post-excise) (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau).
  • Versus the previous quarter call, management commentary shifted from CEO-led, longer-term framing to a CFO-led focus on Q1 delivery, U.S. normalization mechanics, and reconfirming guidance, while analysts continued to concentrate on the same pressure points (U.S. pouch growth, FDA timing, Japan excise volatility).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Prepared-remarks leadership changed: the previous quarter featured "Group CEO Jacek Olczak" opening; this quarter’s prepared remarks were delivered by "Group Chief Financial Officer Emmanuel Babeau" without CEO commentary.
  • Q1 execution contrasted with the prior quarter’s Q1 setup: in February, management said it "anticipate[d] broadly flat year-on-year first quarter organic net revenue and operating income" and guided Q1 adjusted diluted EPS of "$1.80 to $1.85"; in April, the CFO reported "over $10 billion" in net revenues and adjusted diluted EPS of "$1.96."
  • Guidance stayed "reconfirm[ed]" on currency-neutral growth but the currency tailwind and EPS range moved: Q4 guidance cited "$0.28" currency benefit and "$8.39 to $8.54" EPS; Q1 updated to "$0.25" and "$8.36 to $8.51" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau).

Risks and Concerns

  • "While the global economic outlook is uncertain" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), management flagged that "the Middle East conflict had a small impact" and remains "uncertain in both duration and potential impact," with costs including "transport, energy and other input costs."
  • Combustibles risks included "higher level of illicit consumption in certain markets" and "excise increases" driving "a significant industry decline" in Mexico (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), with additional volatility expected from Japan excise timing ("volatility between shipment and adjusted IMS is possible").
  • Regulatory and innovation timing in the U.S. remained a recurring concern: "The authorization of ZYN Ultra via the FDA's nicotine pouch pilot program remains a priority" (Group Chief Financial Officer Babeau), and IQOS ILUMA U.S. authorization was described as a continued engagement area.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a "strong start" driven by international smoke-free momentum and pricing, with IQOS continuing to lead growth and profitability while ZYN U.S. results were shaped by investment and inventory normalization. The company "reconfirm[ed]" its 2026 currency-neutral outlook and updated reported EPS guidance to $8.36-$8.51 with a $0.25 currency tailwind, while repeatedly pointing to second-half comparisons and expected innovation launches as key to improving U.S. dynamics amid regulatory timing uncertainty and excise-driven volatility in Japan.
